summary: Spolsky argues that assigning programmers multiple tasks simultaneously destroys productivity due to the enormous cost of context switching. Using a CPU multitasking analogy, he demonstrates that sequential processing yields faster average completion times than multitasking, and that the penalty grows as task-switch time increases. He then maps this to human programmers, arguing that the mental state required for programming makes context switches extraordinarily expensive — on the order of hours, not seconds. His conclusion is that managers should never assign more than one task at a time and should view their role as removing obstacles so programmers can focus deeply.
key_points: Sequential processing produces lower average completion times than multitasking, even with zero-cost task switches
The penalty for multitasking grows proportionally with task-switch duration, making it catastrophic for high-switch-cost work
Programming requires holding vast mental state in short-term memory, making human task switches take hours rather than seconds
Managers should ensure each programmer works on exactly one thing at a time and should absorb interruptions themselves
A programmer who 'starves' one task to focus on another is actually being more productive than one who tries to juggle both
money_quotes: A programmer coding at full throttle is keeping zillions of things in their head at once: everything from names of variables, data structures, important APIs, the names of utility functions that they wrote and call a lot, even the name of the subdirectory where they store their source code.
The human brain seems to move it out of short-term RAM and swaps it out onto a backup tape where it takes forever to retrieve.

I feel like when I have two programming projects on my plate at once, the task switch time is something like 6 hours. In an 8-hour day, that means multitasking reduces my productivity to 2 hours per day.
You should never let people work on more than one thing at once.
Good managers see their responsibility as removing obstacles so that people can focus on one thing and really get it done.
If you give somebody two things to work on, you should be grateful if they 'starve' one task and only work on one, because they're going to get more stuff done and finish the average task sooner.
key_insight: Because programming requires holding enormous mental state in memory, human task-switching costs are so high that managers should never assign a programmer more than one project at a time.
